text: [S1] Just look at that. Down there on the avenue... absolute gridlock. I’m Marcus Vance, and today on Metro Matters we’re tearing into the big one: Should public transport be 100% free? Not subsidized, not cheap—free for everyone. [S2] It has to be, Marcus. Right now, mobility is pay-to-play. If you’re low income, those fares add up fast, affecting access to jobs and healthcare. Making it free instantly levels the playing field and treats transit as a public service, like a library or a fire department. [S1] But \"free\" is a dangerous marketing term. You think drivers will happily pay higher municipal taxes to subsidize a subway they don't use? Most systems rely on fares for 30 to 50% of their budget. If you wipe that out, the quality tanks; you end up with free trains that never show up because there's no money to fix the signals or clean the cars. [S2] That’s a scare tactic. We already heavily subsidize car travel through road maintenance and environmental cleanup that drivers don't fully pay for. It’s an environmental necessity. If even 20% of those people jumped on a free bus, the carbon footprint of this city would drop like a stone. We can't afford not to do it. [S1] But look at the data. When you remove the cost barrier, you often see a spike in \"non-destination ridership,\" where the system becomes a shelter rather than transport. It drives away the tax-paying commuters you’re trying to attract, leading to a death spiral of no riders and no political will to fund it. Plus, you’d lose 40% of your revenue just to save 5% on collection costs. You’d end up hiking sales taxes, making the poor pay more for milk and diapers. [S2] That is so cynical. text: [S1] Alright, so, picture this. It’s the first of the month. You check your bank app, and boom... there’s 1,000 dollars sitting there. Just... deposited. You didn’t clock in for it, you didn’t sell anything, you didn’t answer to a boss. To the believers, that is the foundation of human dignity. To the skeptics? Well, it’s basically writing a check that our economy can’t cash. I’m Alex Mercer, and today on The Fiscal Future, we're diving into Universal Basic Income with Elena Rostova, who thinks we need a safety net yesterday. Elena, you’re calling UBI an "essential evolution." Why is this so urgent now? [S2] It’s about freedom, Alex. We are standing on the edge of the biggest shift in labor history. Automation isn't just taking factory jobs; it’s taking cognitive jobs like writing code and analyzing legal docs. If we don't decouple survival from traditional labor, we’re going to have a massive class of people who are essentially unemployable. UBI isn't "free money." It’s a dividend on progress. [S1] See, that’s a lovely narrative, but it ignores basic economic reality. You talk about decoupling income from work like it’s a small tweak, but it breaks the fundamental link that drives productivity. If you give everyone 1,000 dollars a month just for breathing, you’re removing the primary motivation to get up and contribute. We already have issues filling service jobs; why would someone flip burgers if they can stay home and scrape by? [S2] That is such a cynical view of human nature. We’re talking about a floor, not a ceiling! People are naturally productive—look at Wikipedia or open-source software. Right now, millions are stuck in survival mode, working three gigs just to pay rent. If you take away the fear of starvation, you don't get laziness; you get innovation and people taking risks to start businesses. [S1] Innovation doesn't pay the national debt, Elena. To fund a UBI of 1,000 dollars a month for every adult, you’re looking at a price tag of roughly 3 trillion dollars a year. That's nearly the entire federal tax revenue. Do you double taxes and crush businesses, or print money and trigger hyperinflation like we saw during the pandemic? [S2] You’re assuming the money disappears, but it goes right back into the economy, increasing the velocity of money. And we shouldn't be taxing labor; we need to tax automation. If a company replaces 500 workers with an algorithm, that algorithm should pay the tax. The wealth is there; it’s just sitting at the very top. [S1] But capital is mobile. If you start taxing algorithms, those servers move to Singapore or Dublin overnight, leaving us with zero revenue. Why not use a scalpel instead of a sledgehammer? We could have targeted welfare tied to work requirements or training. That keeps the labor market tight and helps the poor without giving 1,000 dollars to a hedge fund manager. [S2] Because the targeted approach creates a "welfare trap." If you start making money and suddenly lose your benefits, you’re effectively being taxed at 100 percent. UBI is better because you keep the money even when you get a job. It removes the bureaucracy and treats citizens like shareholders in a successful society. [S1] Shareholders in a bankrupt company, maybe. Look at the pilot programs in Finland—the employment effect was negligible. It didn't turn people into entrepreneurs; it just made them slightly happier while they stayed unemployed. We can’t run a national economy on "good vibes." [S2] "Slightly happier" means stress levels dropped and health outcomes improved! Do you know how much depression and poverty-related illness cost the economy? This is an investment in human capital. If a mother can afford to stay home with her kid, that kid has better outcomes later in life. [S1] At a small scale, sure. But if you scale that to 330 million people, the market adjusts. If everyone suddenly has more disposable income, landlords raise rent and grocery prices creep up. In the end, that 1,000 dollars buys you much less. You’re just chasing your tail. [S2] That’s why you need supply-side policies and rent control alongside it! We have the tools to manage inflation, but we don't have the tools to manage mass social unrest when 30 percent of the country becomes obsolete due to AI. [S1] I think this defeatist attitude is dangerous. We should be talking about upskilling and educational reform, not paying people to give up. If we implemented a national UBI tomorrow, I fear that in 5 years the deficit would be exploded and a massive labor shortage would drive the cost of living through the roof. What's your forecast? [S2] In 5 years, I see poverty essentially eradicated. I see a boom in small businesses and the arts. People are healthier, crime is down, and we've successfully transitioned to an automated economy where humans do the human work and machines do the grunt work. It’s a renaissance. [S1] A nightmare or a renaissance. I guess the jury is still out on whether we can afford it—or if we can’t afford not to do it. Elena Rostova, thanks for coming in. I’m Alex Mercer, and this has been The Fiscal Future. Keep watching the graphs. Goodnight.
